segments.title: Constellation frigate risk grew from requirements and redesign
segments.summary: The live lecture argues that the Constellation/FFG(X) program's low-risk parent-design premise became a redesign and industrial-base problem.
segments.body: The discussion captures the stream's central procurement argument. Dr. Clarke moves from the LCS failure into FFG(X), explains that the Navy wanted a parent-design frigate that could keep up with carrier groups and defend itself independently, then criticizes the way requirements, risk avoidance, displacement growth, and design editing turned the Constellation class into something much less direct than a simple parent-child design.

evidence.note: The transcript moves from LCS failure to FFG(X), describing the need for a frigate that could keep up with carrier groups, defend itself, and contribute to the fleet tactical grid.
The design-candidate discussion says the Navy focused on derisking and parent designs, while several criteria pushed against designs that might have better fit rapid modification.
The program-cost section ties displacement growth to added requirements, mission growth, 30-year procurement assumptions, and changing threats.

The delay section says the post-award design work fell years behind, frames the design as a heavily edited great-grandchild rather than a direct parent-child copy, and links delays to workforce and cost pressures.
